food_name: Barley Ginkgo Sweet Soup (Yi Ren Bai Guo Tang)
aliases: 薏仁白果汤 (yì rén bái guǒ tāng)
薏米白果湯 (yi5 mai5 baak6 gwo2 tong1)

chinese: 薏仁白果汤 (yì rén bái guǒ tāng)
薏米白果湯 (yi5 mai5 baak6 gwo2 tong1)

cuisine: Chinese / Singaporean — Traditional Cantonese Tong Shui
region_of_origin: Cantonese Chinese origin; widely adopted in Singapore and Malaysia
food_subgroup: Traditional Chinese sweet soup (tong shui) — grain-based dessert with cooked Job's tears (coix), ginkgo nuts, and lotus seeds in sweetened pandan broth

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for barley ginkgo dessert soup. Closest parent concept: sweet soup (FOODON:03315763).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for this specific traditional Chinese dessert soup. Closest parent category: grain-based dessert preparation (A0171).
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for barley ginkgo sweet soup. Not a clinical entity requiring SNOMED mapping.

main_ingredients: cooked Job's tears (Coix lacryma-jobi / yi ren / coix seed)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of barley ginkgo sweet soup weighs approximately 320g, comprising roughly 160g cooked Job's tears, 50g ginkgo nuts, 30g lotus seeds, and 80g lightly sweetened pandan broth.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Coix seed (Job's tears), cooked; Ginkgo nuts, cooked; Lotus seeds, cooked; combined dish estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values derived from weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked Job's tears/coix seed, cooked ginkgo nuts, and cooked lotus seeds, adjusted for the dish composition (160g cooked coix : 50g ginkgo : 30g lotus seeds : 80g sweetened pandan broth per 320g bowl). Rock sugar contribution to sugar and energy included. Cross-referenced with HPB Singapore nutrient data for traditional Chinese dessert soups. No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of coix seed (Job's tears) and analogous grain-based dessert soups — composite estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for coix/Job's tears (~GI 25), lotus seeds (~GI 38), and ginkgo nuts (~GI 52)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed literature on coix lacryma-jobi glycemic response
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this exact dish. Composite GI estimated at ~38 using weighted ingredient proportions: cooked Job's tears/coix GI ~25 (dominant ingredient, 50% by weight of solids), lotus seeds GI ~38, ginkgo nuts GI ~52, rock sugar GI ~65. Weighted composite yields dish GI ~38. Beta-glucan content of coix (~3–4g/bowl) further attenuates postprandial glucose response. GL per 320g bowl = (38 × 51.2g available carbohydrate) / 100 = ~19.5, rounded to 19. Source: University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries; Huang et al. (2018) coix seed glycemic response study.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (~38mg per 320g bowl); favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio
hypertension.guidance: With only ~12mg sodium per 100g (approximately 38mg per bowl), this dish is suitable for hypertensive individuals; the potassium content (~110mg/100g) from Job's tears, lotus seeds, and ginkgo nuts contributes to a favourable K:Na ratio that supports blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~38 (low), GL ~19 per bowl (medium); beta-glucan content attenuates postprandial glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI (~38) driven by coix beta-glucan and the moderate glycemic load (~19 per 320g bowl) make this one of the more diabetes-friendly Chinese dessert soups; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one bowl per serving and account for the ~51g carbohydrate load within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.1g/100g); beta-glucan (~3–4g/bowl) with LDL-lowering properties; zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is low in saturated fat (0.1g/100g), contains no cholesterol, and provides approximately 3–4g of beta-glucan per bowl from Job's tears, which has demonstrated LDL-cholesterol-lowering effects in peer-reviewed studies, making it a heart-favourable dessert choice.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Coixol (anti-inflammatory phytochemical in Job's tears); very low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Job's tears contains coixol, which has demonstrated mild anti-inflammatory activity in animal models; the very low saturated fat content is consistent with an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern, though direct human clinical evidence for rheumatoid arthritis benefit remains limited.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Barley Ginkgo Sweet Soup (Yi Ren Bai Guo Tang / 薏仁白果汤) is a traditional Cantonese tong shui (sweet soup dessert) widely served at Singapore and Malaysian dessert stalls. A standard bowl (~320g) contains approximately 160g cooked Job's tears (Coix lacryma-jobi, 薏仁 yì rén), 50g cooked ginkgo nuts (白果 bái guǒ), 30g lotus seeds, and 80g lightly sweetened pandan-infused broth made with rock sugar. The soup has a mild, subtly sweet flavour with a soft, slightly chewy texture from the coix grains and a delicate nuttiness from the ginkgo nuts. Per 100g the dish provides approximately 78 kcal, 2.5g protein, 0.5g fat, and 16g carbohydrate. Sodium is very low at ~12mg/100g (~38mg per bowl). The glycemic index is approximately 38 (low), yielding a glycemic load of ~19 per bowl (medium), making it one of the more diabetes-friendly Chinese dessert soups. Job's tears is rich in beta-glucan (~3–4g per bowl), which has demonstrated LDL-cholesterol-lowering and postprandial glucose-attenuating effects. Coixol, a phytochemical in Job's tears, has traditional diuretic and anti-inflammatory properties. Ginkgo nuts must be cooked before consumption and should not exceed 10 nuts per day due to ginkgotoxin content. The dish is vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Nutritional data derived from USDA FoodData Central composite estimate for cooked coix seed, ginkgo nuts, and lotus seeds, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore dessert soup data.
common_user_queries: yi ren bai guo tang diabetic safe
is barley ginkgo soup good for diabetes

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for the individual cooked ingredients (Job's tears/coix seed, ginkgo nuts, lotus seeds) with weighted composite calculation for the standard dish composition, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore dessert soup data. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for coix grain and peer-reviewed literature. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and GI values are supported by approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for this composite traditional Chinese dessert soup is not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish as served is not reported in approved sources. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used in this traditional preparation. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — all ingredients are plant-based. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is confirmed zero — no significant carotenoid or retinol sources in this dish. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero — dish is entirely plant-based (vegan). vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero — no vitamin D sources present. GINKGO NUT SAFETY NOTE: ginkgo nuts contain ginkgotoxin (4-methoxypyridoxine) and must always be cooked before consumption; consumption should not exceed 10 nuts per day for adults. GI value of ~38 is a composite analogous estimate based on weighted ingredient GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement of this exact dish has been published. GL per 320g bowl calculated as (38 × 51.2g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 19. Beta-glucan content estimated at 3–4g per 320g bowl based on coix seed beta-glucan literature values.
